title: Streaming Pattern Matching (Log Monitoring)
description: You receive a stream of characters (one by one). Given a pattern p, report all indices where p ends in the stream, without storing entire stream (bounded memory). Example: stream=a b a b a, p=aba -> matches end at indices 2 and 4.

examples.explanation:
Initialize: buffer=[], m=3, idx=0
1. Receive 'a': buffer=['a'], idx=1, buffer size=1<3, no compare
2. Receive 'b': buffer=['a','b'], idx=2, buffer size=2<3, no compare
3. Receive 'a': buffer=['a','b','a'], idx=3, compare buffer[0:3]='aba' with pattern 'aba': MATCH at idx=3, output 2 (0-indexed end position)
4. Receive 'b': buffer=['b','a','b'] (remove oldest 'a'), idx=4, compare buffer[0:3]='bab' with 'aba': no match
5. Receive 'a': buffer=['a','b','a'] (remove oldest 'b'), idx=5, compare buffer[0:3]='aba' with 'aba': MATCH at idx=5, output 4
Final output: [2, 4]

Initialize: buffer=[], m=3, idx=0
1. 'x': buffer=['x'], idx=1, size=1<3
2. 'y': buffer=['x','y'], idx=2, size=2<3
3. 'z': buffer=['x','y','z'], idx=3, compare 'xyz' with 'xyz': MATCH at idx=3, output 2
4. 'x': buffer=['y','z','x'], idx=4, compare 'yzx' with 'xyz': no match
5. 'y': buffer=['z','x','y'], idx=5, compare 'zxy' with 'xyz': no match
6. 'z': buffer=['x','y','z'], idx=6, compare 'xyz' with 'xyz': MATCH at idx=6, output 5
Final output: [2, 5]

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*m)
approaches.summary: For each new character in the stream, compare the pattern p against the last m characters of the stream, where m is the pattern length, by storing the last m characters in a buffer. This approach is simple but inefficient for long streams.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Streaming Pattern Matching with Brute Force
ladders.desc: Implement complete streaming pattern matching using brute force approach with bounded memory.
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach to streaming pattern matching works by maintaining a sliding window of the last m characters (where m is pattern length) as new characters arrive in the stream. For each new character, we add it to our buffer (removing the oldest if buffer is full), then compare the pattern against this buffer. The intuition is that we only need to check if the pattern matches at the current ending position - we don't need to store the entire stream history. This works because pattern matches can only end at the current position, and we have the last m characters needed to verify such a match. The algorithm processes characters one by one: 1) Receive new character c, 2) Update circular buffer to store last m characters, 3) If buffer has at least m characters, compare pattern p against buffer positions, 4) If all characters match, output current index. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is stream length - for each of n characters, we potentially do m comparisons. Space is O(m) for the buffer. Connection to L1: This uses the pattern comparison subroutine from L1 repeatedly. Common mistakes include: forgetting to handle streams shorter than pattern, incorrect buffer indexing, and off-by-one errors in match reporting. Real code pattern: Use a list or deque as circular buffer, compare using simple loop.

examples.trace:
Initialize: buffer=[], m=3, idx=0
1. Receive 'a': buffer=['a'], idx=1, buffer size=1<3, no compare
2. Receive 'b': buffer=['a','b'], idx=2, buffer size=2<3, no compare
3. Receive 'a': buffer=['a','b','a'], idx=3, compare buffer[0:3]='aba' with pattern 'aba': MATCH at idx=3, output 2 (0-indexed end position)
4. Receive 'b': buffer=['b','a','b'] (remove oldest 'a'), idx=4, compare buffer[0:3]='bab' with 'aba': no match
5. Receive 'a': buffer=['a','b','a'] (remove oldest 'b'), idx=5, compare buffer[0:3]='aba' with 'aba': MATCH at idx=5, output 4
Final output: [2, 4]

Initialize: buffer=[], m=3, idx=0
1. 'x': buffer=['x'], idx=1, size=1<3
2. 'y': buffer=['x','y'], idx=2, size=2<3
3. 'z': buffer=['x','y','z'], idx=3, compare 'xyz' with 'xyz': MATCH at idx=3, output 2
4. 'x': buffer=['y','z','x'], idx=4, compare 'yzx' with 'xyz': no match
5. 'y': buffer=['z','x','y'], idx=5, compare 'zxy' with 'xyz': no match
6. 'z': buffer=['x','y','z'], idx=6, compare 'xyz' with 'xyz': MATCH at idx=6, output 5
Final output: [2, 5]

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pattern Comparison Against Buffer
ladders.desc: Compare a pattern string against a character buffer to check for exact match.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core comparison operation that checks if the pattern matches the current buffer contents. The intuition is straightforward: we need to verify character-by-character equality between two sequences. Given a pattern p of length m and a buffer containing the last m characters from the stream, we compare each position i from 0 to m-1. If all characters match, return True; otherwise return False at first mismatch. This works because we're checking for exact string matching at a specific position. The algorithm: 1) If buffer length < m, return False (can't match), 2) For i from 0 to m-1: if buffer[i] ≠ p[i], return False, 3) Return True if loop completes. Time: O(m) for the comparison. Space: O(1) additional space. Connection to L0: This comparison is called for each new character in the stream. Common mistakes: not checking buffer length first, using wrong indexing, or early termination logic errors. Real code pattern: Simple for-loop with direct character comparison.

examples.trace:
m=3, buffer length=3≥3
Compare position 0: buffer[0]='a' vs p[0]='a': match
Position 1: buffer[1]='b' vs p[1]='b': match
Position 2: buffer[2]='a' vs p[2]='a': match
All positions match → return True

m=3, buffer length=3≥3
Position 0: 'x' vs 'x': match
Position 1: 'y' vs 'y': match
Position 2: 'z' vs 'w': MISMATCH
Return False immediately

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Last m Characters with Bounded Memory
ladders.desc: Implement a sliding window buffer that stores only the most recent m characters from a stream.
ladders.explanation: The core insight for streaming problems with bounded memory is that we only need to remember the last m characters where m is the pattern length. This works because to check if a pattern ends at the current position, we only need the m characters ending at that position. We implement this using a circular buffer or queue that automatically discards old data. The intuition: as new characters arrive, we add them to our storage, and if we already have m characters, we remove the oldest one (first-in-first-out). This ensures we always have exactly the last m characters (or fewer if stream hasn't delivered m yet). The algorithm: 1) Initialize empty buffer, 2) For each new character c: append c to buffer, 3) If buffer length > m: remove first element, 4) Buffer now contains last m characters. Time: O(1) per character (amortized). Space: O(m). Connection to L1: This buffer provides the input for pattern comparison. Common mistakes: using fixed-size array without proper rotation, incorrect removal logic, or not handling initial phase when buffer has < m characters. Real code pattern: Use collections.deque with maxlen=m, or implement circular buffer with index modulo.

examples.output: Buffer after each step showing last 3 chars
examples.trace:
Start: buffer=[]
1. 'a': buffer=['a'] (length=1)
2. 'b': buffer=['a','b'] (length=2)
3. 'c': buffer=['a','b','c'] (length=3, now full)
4. 'd': buffer=['b','c','d'] (remove 'a', add 'd')
5. 'e': buffer=['c','d','e'] (remove 'b', add 'e')
Final buffer contains last 3 chars: ['c','d','e']

ladders.title: Building Block — Processing a Stream of Characters
ladders.desc: Read and process individual characters from a simulated stream one at a time.
ladders.explanation: This building block handles the fundamental challenge of working with streaming data: we receive data incrementally rather than having all data at once. The intuition is that we need to process each character as it arrives, maintaining any necessary state between characters. In real systems, this could be from network packets, file reads, or user input. We simulate this with a function that yields characters or with a loop that processes an iterator. The algorithm: 1) Initialize any state variables, 2) While there are characters in stream: get next character c, 3) Process c (update state, perform checks), 4) Continue until stream ends. Time: O(n) where n is stream length. Space: depends on state maintained. Connection to L2: This provides the character sequence that feeds into the buffer maintenance logic. Common mistakes: not handling end-of-stream properly, assuming all data is available upfront, or incorrect iteration logic. Real code pattern: Use for-loop over character sequence or while with iterator.

examples.trace:
Initialize: count=0
1. Receive 'h': count=1, process 'h'
2. Receive 'e': count=2, process 'e'
3. Receive 'l': count=3, process 'l'
4. Receive 'l': count=4, process 'l'
5. Receive 'o': count=5, process 'o'
Stream ends, final count=5

Initialize: result=''
1. Receive '1': result='1'
2. Receive '2': result='12'
3. Receive '3': result='123'
Final result='123'

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character Comparison and String Indexing
ladders.desc: Compare individual characters and access string elements by position.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves two key skills: comparing if two characters are equal, and accessing specific positions in a string. The intuition is simple: strings are sequences of characters, and we can examine them position by position. Character comparison uses equality operator (== in most languages) to check if two characters are identical. String indexing allows retrieving the character at a specific position (usually 0-based). This works because strings are typically implemented as arrays of characters. The algorithm for comparing two strings character-by-character: 1) Check if lengths equal, 2) For each position i: if str1[i] ≠ str2[i], return False, 3) Return True if all match. Time: O(min(len1, len2)). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is used when processing stream characters against pattern characters. Common mistakes: off-by-one indexing errors, confusing 0-based vs 1-based indexing, or not handling different string lengths. Real code pattern: Direct character access with [] and comparison with ==.

examples.trace:
Lengths both 3
Position 0: 'c' == 'c': match
Position 1: 'a' == 'a': match
Position 2: 't' == 't': match
All match → True

Lengths both 3
Position 0: 'd' == 'd': match
Position 1: 'o' == 'i': MISMATCH
Return False immediately

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Learn that strings are ordered collections of characters with positions.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept introduces strings as sequences of characters where each character has a position. The intuition: a string like 'hello' is not just a word but actually 5 characters in specific order: position 0='h', 1='e', 2='l', 3='l', 4='o'. This ordering matters for pattern matching. Strings have length (number of characters) and support operations like accessing individual characters. This works because computers store strings as arrays of character codes. Key ideas: 1) Strings are immutable sequences, 2) Positions are usually 0-indexed (first character at position 0), 3) Length is number of characters, 4) Empty string has length 0. Connection to L4: This understanding enables character comparison and indexing operations. Common beginner mistakes: thinking of strings as atomic units rather than sequences, confusion about indexing starting at 0 vs 1, or not understanding that spaces are characters too. Real code pattern: len(s) gives length, s[i] gives character at position i.

examples.trace:
Length: 4 characters
Position 0: 'c'
Position 1: 'o'
Position 2: 'd'
Position 3: 'e'
The string is the sequence: 'c' → 'o' → 'd' → 'e'

String 'ab': positions 0='a', 1='b'
String 'ba': positions 0='b', 1='a'
They are different because:
Position 0: 'a' vs 'b' → different
Position 1: 'b' vs 'a' → different
So 'ab' ≠ 'ba'

approaches.name: Rolling Hash O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a rolling hash function to compute the hash of the pattern and the last m characters of the stream efficiently as new characters arrive. Report a match when the hash matches and verify with a direct comparison to handle collisions. This reduces time complexity by avoiding full string comparisons each time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Streaming Pattern Matching with Rolling Hash
ladders.desc: Complete solution to detect pattern matches in a character stream using rolling hash with collision handling.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the full streaming pattern matching problem using the rolling hash approach. The intuition is to maintain a hash of the last m characters (where m is pattern length) as new characters arrive, allowing us to check for pattern matches in O(1) time per character after initial setup. We use a polynomial rolling hash function with a large prime base to minimize collisions, but still perform direct string comparison when hashes match to handle collisions. The algorithm processes characters one by one: for each new character, we update the hash of the current window using the rolling property (remove oldest character, add newest), compare with pattern hash, and if they match, verify with direct comparison before reporting. This achieves O(n) time complexity where n is stream length, and O(m) space for storing the pattern and potentially the last m characters for verification. The connection to L1 is that this uses the rolling hash update function as its core subroutine. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle modulo arithmetic properly (negative remainders), not verifying hash matches with direct comparison (leading to false positives), and incorrect window management when stream length < m. Real code pattern involves maintaining current hash, pattern hash, and a buffer of last m characters.

examples.trace:
Initialize: pattern='aba', m=3, base=31, mod=10^9+7. Compute pattern_hash = (('a'*31^2 + 'b'*31^1 + 'a'*31^0) mod mod) = (97*961 + 98*31 + 97) mod mod = (93217 + 3038 + 97) mod mod = 96352. Initialize current_hash=0, window_buffer=[].
Step 1: char='a', idx=0. window_buffer=['a'], length=1 < m=3, skip.
Step 2: char='b', idx=1. window_buffer=['a','b'], length=2 < 3, skip.
Step 3: char='a', idx=2. window_buffer=['a','b','a'], length=3. Compute current_hash = (('a'*31^2 + 'b'*31^1 + 'a'*31^0) mod mod) = 96352. current_hash == pattern_hash? Yes (96352 == 96352). Verify: window_buffer ['a','b','a'] == pattern 'aba'? Yes. Report match ending at index 2.
Step 4: char='b', idx=3. Remove oldest 'a': current_hash = (current_hash - 'a'*31^2) mod mod = (96352 - 97*961) mod mod = (96352 - 93217) mod mod = 3135. Multiply by base: 3135 * 31 = 97185. Add new 'b': 97185 + 98 = 97283. window_buffer=['b','a','b']. current_hash=97283 != pattern_hash=96352, skip.
Step 5: char='a', idx=4. Remove oldest 'b': current_hash = (97283 - 98*31^2) mod mod = (97283 - 98*961) mod mod = (97283 - 94178) mod mod = 3105. Multiply by base: 3105 * 31 = 96255. Add new 'a': 96255 + 97 = 96352. window_buffer=['a','b','a']. current_hash=96352 == pattern_hash. Verify: window_buffer ['a','b','a'] == pattern 'aba'? Yes. Report match ending at index 4.
Final output: [2, 4].

Initialize: pattern='xyz', m=3, base=31, mod=10^9+7. pattern_hash = (('x'*31^2 + 'y'*31^1 + 'z'*31^0) mod mod) = (120*961 + 121*31 + 122) mod mod = (115320 + 3751 + 122) mod mod = 119193. Initialize current_hash=0, window_buffer=[].
Step 1: char='x', idx=0. window_buffer=['x'], length=1 < 3, skip.
Step 2: char='y', idx=1. window_buffer=['x','y'], length=2 < 3, skip.
Step 3: char='z', idx=2. window_buffer=['x','y','z'], length=3. current_hash = (('x'*31^2 + 'y'*31^1 + 'z'*31^0) mod mod) = 119193. current_hash == pattern_hash? Yes (119193 == 119193). Verify: window_buffer ['x','y','z'] == pattern 'xyz'? Yes. Report match ending at index 2.
Step 4: char='x', idx=3. Remove oldest 'x': current_hash = (119193 - 120*961) mod mod = (119193 - 115320) mod mod = 3873. Multiply by base: 3873 * 31 = 120063. Add new 'x': 120063 + 120 = 120183. window_buffer=['y','z','x']. current_hash=120183 != pattern_hash=119193, skip.
Step 5: char='y', idx=4. Remove oldest 'y': current_hash = (120183 - 121*31^2) mod mod = (120183 - 121*961) mod mod = (120183 - 116281) mod mod = 3902. Multiply by base: 3902 * 31 = 120962. Add new 'y': 120962 + 121 = 121083. window_buffer=['z','x','y']. current_hash=121083 != pattern_hash, skip.
Step 6: char='z', idx=5. Remove oldest 'z': current_hash = (121083 - 122*31^2) mod mod = (121083 - 122*961) mod mod = (121083 - 117242) mod mod = 3841. Multiply by base: 3841 * 31 = 119071. Add new 'z': 119071 + 122 = 119193. window_buffer=['x','y','z']. current_hash=119193 == pattern_hash. Verify: window_buffer ['x','y','z'] == pattern 'xyz'? Yes. Report match ending at index 5.
Final output: [2, 5].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Rolling Hash Update Function
ladders.desc: Function to update hash when sliding window moves by one character in O(1) time.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the rolling hash approach, enabling efficient hash updates as the window slides. The intuition is that when we move from window [c1, c2, ..., cm] to [c2, c3, ..., cm+1], we can compute the new hash from the old hash without recomputing from scratch, using the polynomial structure of the hash function. Given hash H_old = (c1*b^(m-1) + c2*b^(m-2) + ... + cm*b^0) mod p, to get H_new for window starting at c2: first subtract the contribution of c1 (c1*b^(m-1)), then multiply the remaining hash by base b (which shifts all exponents up by 1), then add the new character cm+1. This works because: H_new = (c2*b^(m-1) + c3*b^(m-2) + ... + cm+1*b^0) = ((H_old - c1*b^(m-1)) * b + cm+1) mod p. The algorithm requires precomputing b^(m-1) mod p for efficiency. Time complexity is O(1) per update (constant arithmetic operations). Space is O(1) for storing current hash and precomputed power. Connection to L0: this function is called for each new character in the stream to update the window hash. Common mistakes include incorrect modulo arithmetic (especially with negative numbers), forgetting to multiply by base after removal, and using integer overflow instead of modulo. Real code pattern: def update_hash(old_hash, old_char, new_char, base_power, base, mod): new_hash = (old_hash - old_char*base_power) % mod; new_hash = (new_hash * base) % mod; new_hash = (new_hash + new_char) % mod; return new_hash.

examples.input: Old window: 'a','b','c' (ASCII 97,98,99), old_hash=1000, base=31, base_power=31^2=961, mod=10007, new_char='d' (ASCII 100)
Old window: 'x','y' (m=2), old_hash=500, base=31, base_power=31^1=31, mod=1009, new_char='z' (ASCII 120,121,122)

testCases.input: old_hash=100, old_char=97 ('a'), new_char=98 ('b'), base_power=31, base=31, mod=10007
old_hash=0, old_char=120 ('x'), new_char=121 ('y'), base_power=1, base=31, mod=1009
old_hash=5000, old_char=65 ('A'), new_char=66 ('B'), base_power=961, base=31, mod=10007
old_hash=1234, old_char=48 ('0'), new_char=49 ('1'), base_power=31, base=31, mod=1009
old_hash=9999, old_char=97 ('a'), new_char=97 ('a'), base_power=31, base=31, mod=10007
old_hash=100, old_char=10, new_char=20, base_power=5, base=7, mod=13
old_hash=50, old_char=2, new_char=3, base_power=4, base=5, mod=17
old_hash=0, old_char=0, new_char=5, base_power=1, base=10, mod=100
old_hash=12, old_char=3, new_char=4, base_power=2, base=3, mod=7
old_hash=1000000, old_char=255, new_char=128, base_power=1000, base=257, mod=1000000007

ladders.title: Core Logic — Polynomial Rolling Hash Function
ladders.desc: Compute hash of a string as a polynomial in a chosen base modulo a large prime.
ladders.explanation: This core logic defines the hash function used to represent strings as numbers, enabling efficient comparison. The intuition is to treat a string as coefficients of a polynomial evaluated at a base b, then take modulo a large prime p to keep numbers manageable. For string s of length m with characters c1..cm (ASCII values), hash = (c1*b^(m-1) + c2*b^(m-2) + ... + cm*b^0) mod p. This creates a fingerprint of the string: different strings likely have different hashes (especially with large b and p), but collisions can occur. The polynomial structure allows rolling updates: when we shift the window, we can update hash in O(1) instead of O(m). Algorithm: initialize hash=0, for each character ci in string: hash = (hash * b + ci) mod p. This builds the polynomial from left to right. Time complexity O(m) to compute hash for length m string. Space O(1). Connection to L1: this hash computation is used to get initial pattern hash and initial window hash; the rolling update function relies on this polynomial representation. Common mistakes include using small base/prime leading to frequent collisions, not using modulo properly causing overflow, and incorrect direction (right-to-left vs left-to-right). Real code pattern: def compute_hash(s, base, mod): hash_val = 0; for ch in s: hash_val = (hash_val * base + ord(ch)) % mod; return hash_val.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize hash=0. Process 'a' (ASCII 97): hash = (0 * 31 + 97) mod 10007 = 97 mod 10007 = 97. Step 2: Process 'b' (ASCII 98): hash = (97 * 31 + 98) mod 10007 = (3007 + 98) mod 10007 = 3105 mod 10007 = 3105. Verify polynomial: 'a'*31^1 + 'b'*31^0 = 97*31 + 98 = 3007 + 98 = 3105. Match.

ladders.title: Building Block — Modular Arithmetic for Hashing
ladders.desc: Perform arithmetic operations (add, multiply, subtract) modulo a prime to prevent integer overflow.
ladders.explanation: This building block covers the modular arithmetic needed to compute hashes within fixed bounds, preventing integer overflow and keeping numbers manageable. The intuition is that when working with large numbers from polynomial evaluation, we take results modulo a large prime p, which maps numbers to range [0, p-1]. This maintains the property that if a ≡ b mod p, then a and b have same remainder when divided by p. For hash collisions: if two strings have same hash mod p, they may or may not be equal. Operations: addition: (a + b) mod p = ((a mod p) + (b mod p)) mod p; multiplication: (a * b) mod p = ((a mod p) * (b mod p)) mod p; subtraction: (a - b) mod p = ((a mod p) - (b mod p)) mod p (handle negative by adding p). Algorithm involves applying mod after each operation to keep numbers small. Time complexity O(1) per operation. Space O(1). Connection to L2: modular arithmetic is used in compute_hash and update_hash functions. Common mistakes include forgetting mod after multiplication (causing overflow), incorrect handling of negative numbers in subtraction, and using non-prime modulus which reduces uniformity. Real code pattern: In Python, use % operator; for subtraction: (a - b) % p gives non-negative result automatically.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute 97*961 = 93217. Step 2: Subtract: 5000 - 93217 = -88217. Step 3: Mod 10007: need non-negative remainder. Compute -88217 mod 10007. Add multiples of 10007 until non-negative: -88217 + 9*10007 = -88217 + 90063 = 1846. Check: 1846 is between 0 and 10006. Alternatively, in code: (-88217) % 10007 = 1846 (Python gives this). So output 1846. Wait, let's verify: 5000 - 93217 = -88217 indeed. -88217 / 10007 ≈ -8.81, so add 9*10007: -88217 + 90063 = 1846. Yes.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Sliding Window of Fixed Size
ladders.desc: Maintain a window of last m characters from a stream as new characters arrive.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves keeping track of the most recent m characters from an incoming stream, which is necessary for comparing against the pattern of length m. The intuition is to use a data structure (like a list or deque) that stores characters, and when a new character arrives, we add it to the end and remove the oldest if window size exceeds m. This maintains a sliding window that moves one step at a time. Algorithm: initialize empty window; for each new character: append to window; if window length > m, remove first character; now window contains last m characters (or fewer if stream length < m). Time complexity O(1) per character for append/remove if using deque, O(m) if using list with shift. Space O(m) to store window. Connection to L3: this window is used to store characters for direct comparison when hash matches, and to know which character is being removed in rolling hash update. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in window size, not handling initial phase when window length < m, and inefficient removal from front of list. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; window = deque(maxlen=m); for ch in stream: window.append(ch); # automatically discards oldest if exceeds m.

examples.trace: Initialize window = []. Step 1: char='a', append: window=['a'], length=1 ≤ 3, keep. Step 2: char='b', append: window=['a','b'], length=2 ≤ 3. Step 3: char='c', append: window=['a','b','c'], length=3 ≤ 3. Step 4: char='d', append: window=['a','b','c','d'], length=4 > 3, so remove first: window becomes ['b','c','d'].
Window = []. Step 1: 'x' -> ['x'], length=1 ≤ 2. Step 2: 'y' -> ['x','y'], length=2 ≤ 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Character to Integer Mapping
ladders.desc: Convert characters to numerical values (e.g., ASCII codes) for use in mathematical computations.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves mapping characters to integers so they can be used in hash computations. The intuition is that computers represent characters as numbers (e.g., ASCII or Unicode), and we need these numerical representations to perform arithmetic operations like addition and multiplication in hash functions. For example, in ASCII, 'a' is 97, 'b' is 98, etc. Algorithm: use a function like ord(ch) in Python to get integer code of character; for simplicity, we often use ASCII values which range 0-127 for standard characters. Time complexity O(1) per character. Space O(1). Connection to L4: when adding characters to window or computing hashes, we use their integer values. Common mistakes include assuming all characters are lowercase letters, not handling non-ASCII characters, and confusing character with its string representation. Real code pattern: value = ord('a') # returns 97.

examples.trace: In ASCII encoding, the character 'a' corresponds to decimal value 97. This is standard in most programming languages when using ord().
The digit '0' has ASCII value 48, '1' is 49, up to '9' is 57. So ord('0') returns 48.

approaches.name: KMP Automaton O(n)
approaches.summary: Preprocess the pattern to build a finite automaton (or failure function) that tracks the longest prefix of the pattern that is also a suffix of the stream processed so far. As each character arrives, update the state to efficiently detect matches without re-scanning the stream. This ensures linear time without hash collisions.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Streaming Pattern Matching with KMP Automaton
ladders.desc: Complete solution to detect pattern matches in a character stream using KMP automaton with bounded memory.
ladders.explanation: This solution addresses the core challenge of pattern matching in a streaming context where we cannot store the entire stream. The KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) automaton approach preprocesses the pattern to build a failure function (or automaton transition table) that encodes the longest prefix of the pattern that is also a suffix of the processed stream. As each character arrives from the stream, we update a state variable using this automaton. The state represents how much of the pattern matches the suffix of the stream processed so far. When the state equals the pattern length, we've found a match ending at the current index. The intuition is that instead of restarting from the beginning of the pattern after a mismatch (like naive matching), we use the failure function to jump to the longest prefix that still matches, avoiding redundant comparisons. This ensures O(n) time complexity where n is the stream length, and O(m) space for the automaton where m is the pattern length. The algorithm processes the stream character by character: initialize state to 0, for each incoming character, while state > 0 and stream_char != pattern[state], set state = failure[state-1] (using the failure function to backtrack), then if stream_char == pattern[state], increment state. If state == m, record a match at current index and set state = failure[state-1] to continue searching for overlapping matches. The failure function is built by preprocessing the pattern: initialize failure[0]=0, for i from 1 to m-1, set j = failure[i-1], while j>0 and pattern[i]!=pattern[j], set j = failure[j-1], then if pattern[i]==pattern[j], failure[i]=j+1 else 0. This captures the longest proper prefix which is also a suffix for each prefix of the pattern. Common mistakes include not handling overlapping matches correctly (by resetting state to failure[state-1] after a match) or incorrect failure function construction. The connection to L1 is that this level integrates the failure function building (L1) with the streaming matching logic.

examples.trace: Preprocess pattern 'aba': failure=[0,0,1]. Process stream: index 0, char 'a', state=0, match 'a' with pattern[0]='a', state=1. index 1, char 'b', state=1, match 'b' with pattern[1]='b', state=2. index 2, char 'a', state=2, match 'a' with pattern[2]='a', state=3 (equals pattern length), record match at index 2, set state=failure[2]=1. index 3, char 'b', state=1, match 'b' with pattern[1]='b', state=2. index 4, char 'a', state=2, match 'a' with pattern[2]='a', state=3, record match at index 4, set state=1. Output indices [2,4].

Preprocess pattern 'aa': failure=[0,1]. Process stream: index 0, char 'a', state=0, match 'a' with pattern[0]='a', state=1. index 1, char 'a', state=1, match 'a' with pattern[1]='a', state=2 (match), record index 1, set state=failure[1]=1. index 2, char 'a', state=1, match 'a' with pattern[1]='a', state=2, record index 2, set state=1. index 3, char 'a', state=1, match 'a' with pattern[1]='a', state=2, record index 3, set state=1. Output [1,2,3].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building the KMP Failure Function
ladders.desc: Construct the failure function (longest prefix-suffix array) for a given pattern.
ladders.explanation: The failure function is the core preprocessing step in KMP that enables efficient pattern matching. For a pattern of length m, we compute an array failure of length m where failure[i] is the length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix of pattern[0..i]. Proper prefix means it cannot be the entire string itself. This function captures self-overlaps within the pattern, allowing us to skip unnecessary comparisons during matching. The intuition is that if we have matched up to position i in the pattern and then encounter a mismatch, instead of restarting from the beginning, we can jump to failure[i-1] because that prefix is already known to match the suffix of what we've processed. The algorithm: initialize failure[0]=0 (since a single character has no proper prefix). For i from 1 to m-1, set j = failure[i-1]. While j > 0 and pattern[i] != pattern[j], set j = failure[j-1] (backtrack to the next best prefix). If pattern[i] == pattern[j], set failure[i] = j+1, else set failure[i]=0. This iterative process builds the array in O(m) time by reusing previously computed values. Space complexity is O(m) for the array. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indices or incorrect backtracking logic. The connection to L0 is that this failure function is used in the streaming matching algorithm to update states efficiently.

examples.trace: m=3, failure[0]=0. i=1, pattern[1]='b', j=failure[0]=0, j>0? no, pattern[1]!='a', so failure[1]=0. i=2, pattern[2]='a', j=failure[1]=0, j>0? no, pattern[2]=='a'? compare with pattern[0]='a': yes, so failure[2]=0+1=1. Result [0,0,1].

Step-by-step: failure[0]=0. i=1: j=0, pattern[1]=='a' matches pattern[0], failure[1]=1. i=2: j=1, pattern[2]=='b' != pattern[1]='a', backtrack j=failure[0]=0, pattern[2]!='a', failure[2]=0. i=3: j=0, pattern[3]=='a' matches pattern[0], failure[3]=1. i=4: j=1, pattern[4]=='a' matches pattern[1], failure[4]=2. i=5: j=2, pattern[5]=='b' matches pattern[2], failure[5]=3. i=6: j=3, pattern[6]=='a' matches pattern[3], failure[6]=4. i=7: j=4, pattern[7]=='a' matches pattern[4], failure[7]=5. i=8: j=5, pattern[8]=='a' != pattern[5]='b', backtrack j=failure[4]=2, pattern[8]=='a' != pattern[2]='b', backtrack j=failure[1]=1, pattern[8]=='a' matches pattern[1]='a', failure[8]=2. Final array [0,1,0,1,2,3,4,5,2].

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transition in KMP Automaton
ladders.desc: Update the matching state using the failure function when processing a new character.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the dynamic state update mechanism that makes KMP efficient for streaming. We maintain a state variable that represents the length of the longest prefix of the pattern that matches the suffix of the stream processed so far. When a new character arrives, we need to update this state. The key insight is to use the failure function to handle mismatches without restarting from scratch. The algorithm: given current state and a new character c, while state > 0 and c != pattern[state], set state = failure[state-1] (backtrack using the failure function). This while loop finds the largest prefix that still matches after considering c. Then, if c == pattern[state], increment state by 1. If state equals the pattern length m, we've found a match. The intuition is that the failure function tells us, for each prefix length, where to fall back if the next character doesn't match, allowing us to skip re-checking characters. This ensures each character from the stream is processed in O(1) amortized time, leading to O(n) total time. Space is O(1) for the state variable. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle the case when state becomes 0 or incorrect comparison indices. The connection to L0 is that this state transition is repeated for each stream character in the full solution.

examples.trace: Start state=2. Check: state>0? yes, new_char='a' vs pattern[2]='a': they match, so skip while loop. Since they match, increment state to 3. State equals pattern length 3, so match detected.

Start state=4. While loop: state>0? yes, new_char='a' vs pattern[4]='c': mismatch, so set state=failure[3]=2. Now state=2, check: state>0? yes, new_char='a' vs pattern[2]='a': match, exit loop. Since match, increment state to 3? Wait: after loop, we check if new_char matches pattern[state]: new_char='a' vs pattern[2]='a' matches, so state becomes 3? Correction: in the algorithm, after the while loop, if c == pattern[state], then state++. Here, after backtracking to state=2, c='a' matches pattern[2]='a', so state becomes 3. But let's re-trace carefully: initial state=4, c='a' != pattern[4]='c', so state=failure[3]=2. Now state=2, c='a' == pattern[2]='a', so state becomes 3. Output state=3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Longest Prefix-Suffix Computation
ladders.desc: Compute the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix for a given string.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is fundamental to building the KMP failure function. For a string s of length k, we want to find the longest proper prefix (a prefix that is not the entire string) that is also a suffix of s. For example, for s='abab', the longest proper prefix that is also a suffix is 'ab' of length 2. This property is key because it helps in pattern matching by identifying how much of the pattern can be reused after a mismatch. The intuition is that if we know this overlap, we can skip re-comparing characters that are already known to match. The algorithm can be implemented by checking prefixes and suffixes directly: for length l from k-1 down to 1, check if s[0:l] equals s[k-l:k]. This takes O(k^2) time in the worst case, but for KMP, we use a more efficient O(k) method as in L1. However, at this level, we focus on understanding the concept. Step-by-step for s='abcab': check l=4: prefix 'abca' vs suffix 'bcab' no; l=3: 'abc' vs 'cab' no; l=2: 'ab' vs 'ab' yes, so length 2. Time complexity for this naive approach is O(k^2), space O(1). Common mistakes include not considering proper prefixes or off-by-one in substring indices. The connection to L1 is that this computation is generalized in the failure function for all prefixes of the pattern.

examples.trace: Length k=4. Check l=3: prefix 'aaa' vs suffix 'aaa' match, so output 3.
k=4. Check l=3: 'abc' vs 'bcd' no. l=2: 'ab' vs 'cd' no. l=1: 'a' vs 'd' no. So output 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Characters and Updating Indices
ladders.desc: Compare characters in strings and update indices based on matches or mismatches.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating through strings, comparing characters at specific positions, and adjusting indices accordingly. In the context of pattern matching, we often have two strings: a text (or stream) and a pattern. We maintain indices i for the text and j for the pattern. The basic operation is: if text[i] equals pattern[j], we increment both i and j to continue matching the next characters. If they do not match, we need to decide how to update j (and possibly i) to resume matching. In naive pattern matching, we reset j to 0 and move i back, but in KMP, we use a more sophisticated update using the failure function. The intuition is that character-by-character comparison is the building block of string matching algorithms. The algorithm in pseudocode: initialize i=0, j=0. While i < n and j < m, if text[i] == pattern[j], i++, j++. Else, handle mismatch (e.g., in naive: i = i - j + 1, j=0). This operation has O(1) time per comparison and O(1) space. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds or incorrect reset logic. The connection to L2 is that this basic comparison is enhanced in KMP by using the failure function to update j intelligently during mismatches.

examples.trace: Start i=0, j=0. text[0]='a' == pattern[0]='a', so i=1, j=1. text[1]='b' == pattern[1]='b', so i=2, j=2. j equals pattern length 2, so full match.
Start i=0, j=0. text[0]='a' == pattern[0]='a', i=1, j=1. text[1]='x' != pattern[1]='b', mismatch. In naive matching, reset: i = i - j + 1 = 1 - 1 + 1 = 1, j=0. So i=1, j=0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Prefixes and Suffixes
ladders.desc: Understand prefixes and suffixes of a string and how to identify them.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept for pattern matching algorithms. A string is a sequence of characters. A prefix of a string is any substring that starts at the first character. For example, for the string 'hello', prefixes are 'h', 'he', 'hel', 'hell', 'hello'. A suffix is any substring that ends at the last character, such as 'o', 'lo', 'llo', 'ello', 'hello'. A proper prefix or suffix is one that is not the entire string. Understanding these definitions is crucial because pattern matching often involves comparing prefixes of the pattern with suffixes of the text. The intuition is that when matching strings, we are essentially checking if certain prefixes of the pattern equal suffixes of the text processed so far. To work with prefixes and suffixes, we can use indexing: for a string s of length n, the prefix of length k is s[0:k] (first k characters), and the suffix of length k is s[n-k:n] (last k characters). This operation is O(k) time to extract or compare. Common mistakes include confusing prefixes with substrings or incorrect index calculations. The connection to L3 is that the longest prefix-suffix computation relies on comparing prefixes and suffixes of the same string.

examples.trace: For string 'abc' of length 3, prefix of length 2: take first 2 characters 'ab'. Suffix of length 2: take last 2 characters 'bc'.
String 'test' has length 4. Proper prefixes (not the whole string): length 1: 't', length 2: 'te', length 3: 'tes'. Length 4 is the whole string, so not included.
